---
title: "Package managers: Pixi"
format: 
    html:
      toc: true
      toc-location: left
      page-layout: full
summary: Intro to envs
css: ./web.css
---

```{r,include=FALSE, results='asis'}
source("_setup.R")
```

Put your learning to the test with what you’ve covered so far.

## A. General environment 

::: {.webex-check .callout-exercise}
# I - General knowledge 

```{r, results='asis', echo = FALSE}

opts_1 <- c(
   answer="Automating the execution of complex computational processes",
   "To manually execute each step of a computation",
   "Offering real-time and intuitive data analysis",
   "To minimize the reliance on specific software environments"
)

cat("**G.1. What role does a workflow manager play in computational research??**", longmcq(opts_1))
```

:::

:::{.panel-tabset .tool-tabs}
## <img src="images/ucloud.png" height="50"> 


:::{.callout-note}
For this exercise, you will need to submit two jobs using the Terminal app. 

First job:

- Resources: 1 CPU and 1 hour
- Mount hpcPipes folder and double-check you have mounted the right directory (e.g. `ls`). Do not mount the full personal drive (e.g. YourName###1234). 
- Once the job is running, make sure your working directory for all exercises is `/work/hpcPipes`. 
:::

We will now install pixi and save it to your WD. 

```bash
# Download
curl -fsSL https://pixi.sh/install.sh | bash

# Move pixi 
mkdir -p /work/pixi
mv ~/.pixi/bin /work/hpcPipes/pixi
```

After installation, create an initialization file to update your shell configuration whenever a new terminal session starts. This step is essential in UCloud environments, without it, Pixi will not be available once the running job is closed. Since the Pixi binary has been moved to a custom directory (which you will be mounting every time you start a new job), the initialization file ensures that its location is correctly added to your PATH each time a new session begins.

```{.bash filename="setup_pixi.sh"}
#!/usr/bin

echo 'export PATH="$PATH:/work/hpcpipes/pixi/bin""'>> /home/ucloud/.bashrc

source ~/.bashrc
```

Now, `Stop application` and resubmit a new job:

- Resources: 1 CPU and 1 hour
- Mount hpcPipes folder and double-check you have mounted the right directory (e.g. `ls`). Do not mount the full personal drive (e.g. YourName###1234)
- Additional Parameters. Initialization: `/work/YourName#1234/hpcPipes/setup_pixi.sh`
- Make sure your working directory for all exercises is `/work/hpcPipes`

Unsure which command to use in the exercises below? Check [Pixi documentation](https://pixi.prefix.dev/v0.43.1/) for guidance! 


## <img src="images/genomedk.png" height="50"> 

### Install Pixi

Open a terminal and install Pixi:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://pixi.sh/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, update your shell configuration so the `pixi` command is available in future terminal sessions:

```.bash
echo 'PATH="$PATH:$HOME/.pixi/bin"' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'eval "$(pixi completion --shell bash)"' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

## <img src="images/computer.png" height="50"> 
Follow these [instructions](https://pixi.prefix.dev/dev/installation/): 

```{.bash filename="Linux/Max"}
curl -fsSL https://pixi.sh/install.sh | sh
```

```{.bash filename="Windows"}
wget -qO- https://pixi.sh/install.sh | sh
```
:::


::: {.webex-check .callout-exercise}
# Exercise: Creating and sharing environments with Pixi

In this exercise, you will create a reproducible software environment using **Pixi**, install packages from multiple channels, and export the environment so it can also be recreated with Conda. Check [Pixi documentation](https://pixi.prefix.dev/v0.43.1/) to complete the exercises below. 

What is the pixi version you have installed: `r fitb("^([0-9])\\.[0-9]+\\.[0-9]+$", regex = TRUE)`


### Navigate to your project directory

Move to the working directory you created earlier (**hpcPipes**), and download the `data.fastq`:

```.bash
cd ~/hpcPipes

wget https://github.com/hartwigmedical/testdata/raw/master/100k_reads_hiseq/TESTX/TESTX_H7YRLADXX_S1_L001_R1_001.fastq.gz \
     -O ./data.fastq.gz

gunzip data.fastq.gz

head data.fastq
```

### Initialize a Pixi Project

Create a new Pixi project and configure it to use the `conda-forge` and `bioconda` channels:

```.bash
pixi init --channel conda-forge --channel bioconda
```

Pixi creates a file called `pixi.toml`, which contains the information needed to recreate your environment.

List the files in the directory (`ls`). 

You should now see a new file called `pixi.toml`.

### Install software packages

Ensure you are still in the directory containing `pixi.toml`, then install a collection of commonly used packages:

```.bash
pixi add python r r-ggplot2 r-dplyr pandas
```

### Inspect the configuration

Open `pixi.toml` in your preferred text editor and answer the following questions: 

- How many channels are defined? `r fitb(answer='2')`
- For how many platform has the environment configured? `r fitb(answer='1')`
- Can you add another platform?  `r torf(TRUE)`
- How many tasks are specified? `r fitb(answer='0')`
- How many dependencies are listed? `r fitb(answer='5')`

Now, use the Pixi CLI to add a new platform so that this environment is compatible with macOS. 

:::{.callout-hint}
```.bash
pixi project platform add
```
:::

- Does pixi.toml file now look like this `platform = ["linux-64", "osx-64"]`? `r torf(TRUE)`

Additional channels can be added later if required by specific software packages.

### Add a Bioinformatics Package

Let's install a package from Bioconda, *fastqc*.  What is the *fastqc* version? Use a pixi command to find out! `r fitb("^(v[0-9])\\.[0-9]+\\.[0-9]+$", regex = TRUE)`

This demonstrates how Pixi can install software from the same package ecosystem commonly used with Conda.

### Compare with Conda

Export the environment to a Conda-compatible file:

```bash
pixi project export conda-environment environment.yml
```

Open the generated `environment.yml` file and compare it with `pixi.toml`.

Discuss with your neighbour:

* Which file do you find easier to read?
* What information is stored in each file? 
* Can conda manage environments that are compatible with multiple platforms?

### Recreate the Environment

A collaborator who receives your `pixi.toml` file can recreate the environment with:

```bash
pixi install
```

A collaborator using Conda could instead recreate the environment from the exported file:

```bash
conda create -f environment.yml
```

### Package search

Search for a package that interests you and install it with Pixi.

Examples:

```bash
pixi search samtools
```

```bash
pixi search blast
```

Install one of the packages you find:

```bash
pixi add samtools
```

or

```bash
pixi add blast
```

Verify the installation:

```bash
pixi run samtools --version
```

### Share your environment

Create a compressed archive containing the files needed to reproduce your environment:

```bash
zip environment.zip pixi.toml environment.yml
```

Inspect the archive:

```bash
unzip -l environment.zip
```

You can now share this archive with collaborators, who can recreate the same software environment using either Pixi or Conda.
:::

:::{.callout-hint}
# Solution
```.bash
# Pixi version
pixi --version
# Pixi number of packages
pixi list --json | jq length
# Add macos platform
pixi project platform add osx-64
# Add package fastqc 
pixi add fastqc
# Pixi command, fastqc version
pixi run fastqc --version!
```
Conda does not natively manage a single environment definition that targets multiple platforms at once in the same way tools like Pixi do.
:::


:::{.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Bonus: Creating a multi-platform environment

One advantage of Pixi is that a single environment can be made reproducible across different operating systems.

Check which platforms are currently supported by your project and add support for Linux, macOS, and Windows:

```.bash
pixi project platform list
pixi project platform add linux-64 osx-64 osx-arm64 win-64
```

Verify all platforms have been added: 

```.bash
pixi project platform list
```

Open `pixi.toml` and find the platforms section. 

- Why is a multi-platform environment useful when sharing software with a broader community?
- What challenges might arise if a package is not available on all platforms?

:::

:::{.callout-tip collapse="true"}
# Bonus: Multiple environments in a single project

So far, we have created a single environment containing all required software. In practice, projects often require multiple environments for different purposes.

For example:

* A **production** environment containing only the software required to run an application or analysis.
* A **test** environment containing additional tools used for testing and validation.
* A **development** environment containing debugging and development utilities.
* A **GPU** environment containing libraries that require GPU support.
* A **CPU** environment that can run on systems without GPUs.

Pixi allows multiple environments to coexist within the same project while sharing dependencies whenever possible.

### Challenge 1: CPU & GPU environments

Many machine learning projects need to support both systems with GPUs and systems without GPUs.

A possible setup could look like:

```text
default (CPU)
├── python
├── pandas
├── scikit-learn
└── pytorch (CPU)

gpu
├── python
├── pandas
├── scikit-learn
└── pytorch-cuda
```

This approach allows the same project to run on a laptop, HPC cluster, or cloud system while only installing the software required for the available hardware. Could you build a pixi environment with that setup?

### Challenge 2: Teaching & course environments

Imagine you maintain materials for a bioinformatics course. Participants only need software required to complete the exercises, while course developers need additional tools for creating and testing content.

The following pixi.toml illustrates this idea:

````.toml 
[workspace]
name = "gdk-course"
channels = ["conda-forge", "bioconda"]
platforms = ["linux-64", "osx-arm64"]

[tasks]
launch = "jupyter lab"
build-course = "quarto render"

[dependencies]
python = ">=3.12"
jupyterlab = "*"
pandas = "*"
samtools = "*"
fastqc = "*"

[feature.developer.dependencies]
quarto = "*"
pytest = "*"
ruff = "*"

[feature.developer.tasks]
check = "pytest"

[environments]
default = {features = [], solve-group = "course-group"}
developer = {features = ["developer"], solve-group = "course-group"}
```

In this example:

*default contains only the software needed by workshop participants.
*developer contains everything in default plus tools for creating and testing course material.
*Both environments share the same versions of common packages through the solve-group.

A workshop participant would typically run:
```
pixi run launch
```
A course developer could run:
```
pixi run check
pixi run build-course
```
Build the multi-env in Pixi that supports that specific setup and make sure it works as intended. 

:::
